title: jQuery Intro
content:
jQuery Introduction

Write Less, Do More

jQuery is a lightweight JavaScript library that completely changed web development. Its purpose is to make it much easier to use JavaScript on your website by taking common tasks that require many lines of vanilla JavaScript code, and wrapping them into methods that you can call with a single line of code.



  • Simplifies HTML DOM manipulation and traversal.
  • Makes Event Handling (like button clicks) effortless.
  • Provides built-in animations and effects.
  • Simplifies AJAX calls for seamless background data fetching.

title: Getting Started
content:
Getting Started with jQuery

Including the Library

To use jQuery, you don't need to install anything complex. You simply include the jQuery library on your web page using a standard <script> tag pointing to a Content Delivery Network (CDN) like Google's.

HTML Setup
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
  <!-- Add this inside your <head> tag to load jQuery -->
  <script src="https://ajax.googleapis.com/ajax/libs/jquery/3.7.1/jquery.min.js"></script>
</head>
<body>
  <h1>My Web Page</h1>
</body>
</html>

title: jQuery Syntax
content:
jQuery Syntax

The $ Sign

The basic jQuery syntax is incredibly simple: $(selector).action(). The $ sign is just a shortcut alias for "jQuery". Best practice is to wrap all your code inside a Document Ready event so the code doesn't run before the HTML has finished loading.

Document Ready
$(document).ready(function() {
  
  // All jQuery methods go inside here!
  // For example, hiding all paragraph tags:
  $("p").hide();
  
});

title: Selectors
content:
jQuery Selectors

Finding Elements

jQuery selectors allow you to find HTML elements based on their name, id, classes, types, attributes, and much more. If you know CSS, you already know jQuery selectors!

Selector Examples
$(document).ready(function() {
  
  // Tag Selector: Selects all <button> elements
  $("button").click(function() {
    
    // ID Selector: Selects the element with id="test"
    $("#test").hide();
    
    // Class Selector: Selects all elements with class="intro"
    $(".intro").hide();
    
    // The 'this' keyword: Selects the specific element that was just clicked
    $(this).hide();
    
  });
});

title: Events
content:
jQuery Events

User Interactions

jQuery makes it incredibly easy to handle user interactions like mouse clicks, hovering, and keyboard strokes. You attach an event handler directly to the selected element.

Event Handlers
$(document).ready(function() {
  
  // Triggers when the user hovers their mouse over a div
  $("div").mouseenter(function() {
    console.log("Mouse entered the element!");
  });
  
  // Triggers when the user double-clicks a paragraph
  $("p").dblclick(function() {
    $(this).hide();
  });
  
});

title: Effects
content:
jQuery Effects

Animations Made Easy

Before CSS3 transitions existed, jQuery was the only way to animate a website. It still provides built-in methods for hiding, showing, fading, sliding, and custom animations that work instantly.

Fades and Slides
$(document).ready(function() {
  
  $("#fadeBtn").click(function() {
    // Fades the box in slowly
    $("#box").fadeIn("slow");
  });
  
  $("#slideBtn").click(function() {
    // Slides the panel up to hide it
    $("#panel").slideUp();
  });
  
});

title: DOM Manipulation
content:
DOM Manipulation

Changing the Page

jQuery makes it easy to get and set HTML content and attributes on the fly. This is how you dynamically update a webpage after a user logs in or submits a form.

  • text(): Sets or returns the text content of selected elements.
  • html(): Sets or returns the content (including HTML markup).
  • val(): Sets or returns the value of form input fields.
  • attr(): Used to get or change attribute values (like an image src).
Changing Attributes
$(document).ready(function() {
  $("#changeBtn").click(function() {
    // Change the href attribute of a link dynamically
    $("#myLink").attr("href", "https://mealmitra.com");
    
    // Change the text inside a span
    $("#status").text("Link Updated!");
  });
});

title: AJAX Basics
content:
jQuery AJAX

Background Data

AJAX is the art of exchanging data with a server, and updating parts of a web page without reloading the whole page. jQuery completely simplified this process by replacing the massive, clunky vanilla XMLHttpRequest code with simple, one-line methods.



The $.get Method
$(document).ready(function() {
  $("#loadBtn").click(function() {
    
    // Request data from a URL, then run a callback function when it returns
    $.get("https://api.example.com/data", function(data, status) {
      console.log("Data loaded successfully!");
      // Inject the received data straight into a div
      $("#resultDiv").html(data);
    });
    
  });
});

title: Selectors Deep Dive
content:
jQuery Selectors

Targeting Elements

jQuery selectors let you "find" (select) HTML elements based on tag name, id, class, attributes, and more. They are based entirely on standard CSS selectors, meaning if you know how to style an element in CSS, you already know how to select it in jQuery!



  • $("p") – selects all <p> (paragraph) elements.
  • $("#myId") – selects the single element with the specific ID of "myId".
  • $(".myClass") – selects all elements that share the class "myClass".

title: Syntax in Action
content:
jQuery Syntax

The Action Pattern

The jQuery syntax is designed to make it incredibly easy to select HTML elements and immediately perform actions on them. The universal pattern is always $(selector).action();.

Document Ready Pattern
$(document).ready(function() {
  // We wait for the DOM to load, then we apply our logic
  
  // Select all <p> tags and apply the hide() action
  $("p").hide();
  
  // Select an element by ID and apply a click action
  $("#submitBtn").click(function() {
    alert("Button clicked!");
  });
});

title: Get Content
content:
Get Content with jQuery

Reading the DOM

You can use jQuery methods to easily get or set the content of HTML elements. These methods act as "getters" when you leave the parentheses empty, and "setters" when you pass a value inside them.



  • text() – gets or sets the raw text content (ignores HTML tags).
  • html() – gets or sets the content, including HTML markup.
  • val() – gets or sets the value of form inputs (like text boxes or dropdowns).
Reading Inputs
$(document).ready(function() {
  $("#readBtn").click(function() {
    // Extract the value typed into a text box
    let username = $("#usernameInput").val();
    
    // Output that value into a paragraph
    $("#welcomeMessage").text("Welcome, " + username + "!");
  });
});

title: Hide and Show
content:
jQuery Hide and Show

Controlling Visibility

jQuery provides extremely simple methods to hide or show HTML elements. You can even pass optional parameters into these methods to control animation speed (like "slow", "fast", or milliseconds) and trigger callback functions when the animation finishes.



The Toggle Method
$(document).ready(function() {
  // toggle() automatically checks if an element is hidden or shown
  // If it is hidden, it shows it. If it is shown, it hides it!
  
  $("#menuBtn").click(function() {
    // Toggles the dropdown menu smoothly over 500 milliseconds
    $("#dropdownMenu").toggle(500);
  });
});

title: Final Summary
content:
jQuery Tutorial Summary 🚀

Course Completed! 🏆

Congratulations! You have successfully learned the fundamentals of jQuery. These core concepts allow you to build interactive and dynamic web functionality with just a few lines of code.

  • Targeting: Selecting HTML elements effortlessly using CSS-style Selectors.
  • Interaction: Utilizing basic jQuery syntax to handle user Events (like clicks and hovers).
  • Manipulation: Getting and setting content dynamically with text(), html(), and val().
  • Animation: Applying smooth UI transitions with hide(), show(), and toggle().

Keep practicing with the Live Editor to reinforce your skills!

title: Event Methods
content:
jQuery Event Methods

Reacting to Users

jQuery provides many event methods to react to user interactions such as clicks, mouse movements, keyboard actions, form changes, and more.

  • click() – runs code when an element is clicked.
  • mouseenter() and mouseleave() – run code when the mouse enters or leaves an element.
  • change() – fires when form elements change.
  • focus() and blur() – run when input fields gain or lose focus.
The on() Method
$(document).ready(function() {
  // You can also use on() to attach multiple event handlers efficiently.
  $("input").on({
    focus: function() {
      $(this).css("background-color", "#e8f0fe");
    },
    blur: function() {
      $(this).css("background-color", "white");
    }
  });
});

title: Effects & Animations
content:
jQuery Effects and Animations

Bringing UI to Life

jQuery lets you hide/show elements and create animations easily. These methods often accept optional speed and callback parameters to control timing and post-animation actions.



  • hide(), show(), and toggle() – hide, show, or switch visibility.
  • fadeIn(), fadeOut(), fadeToggle() – fading effects.
  • slideDown(), slideUp(), slideToggle() – sliding effects.
  • animate() – custom animations with CSS property changes.

title: HTML/DOM Setters
content:
jQuery HTML/DOM Get and Set

Dynamic Updates

jQuery makes reading and modifying content and attributes simple. These methods enable dynamic content updates without page reloads.

  • text() – get or set text content.
  • html() – get or set HTML content.
  • val() – get or set the value of form inputs.
  • attr() – get or set element attributes.

title: AJAX Methods
content:
jQuery AJAX Methods

Server Communication

AJAX lets you exchange data with a server without refreshing the page. These methods update parts of the page dynamically based on the server response.



  • $.ajax() – generalized AJAX request.
  • $.get() and $.post() – load or send data with HTTP GET/POST.
  • $.getJSON() – fetch JSON data easily.

title: Traversing
content:
jQuery Traversing

Navigating the Tree

Traversing lets you navigate the DOM tree from one element selection to others. Traversing is useful when you need to manipulate elements based on relationships in the DOM.



  • parent() – finds the parent element.
  • children() – finds direct child elements.
  • next() / prev() – find immediate siblings.
  • find() – find descendants matching a selector.

title: Advanced Summary
content:
jQuery Advanced Summary 🚀

Library Mastered! 🏆

This section completes your jQuery study with event handling, effects and animations, DOM content manipulation, AJAX methods for server communication, and traversing to navigate and manipulate related elements.

These skills help you build rich, interactive front-end applications using jQuery’s concise, cross-browser API.

title: HTML/CSS Methods
content:
jQuery HTML/CSS Manipulation

Dynamic Styling

jQuery provides many methods to manipulate HTML content and CSS properties of selected elements. You can add, remove, or toggle class names, set or return style properties, and insert HTML in various places relative to other elements. These methods help you dynamically update the look and structure of the page without reloading.

title: Class Methods
content:
Add/Remove/Toggle Classes

Modifying Classes

jQuery makes it easy to modify classes on selected elements:

  • addClass() – Adds one or more class names to selected elements
  • removeClass() – Removes class names
  • toggleClass() – Adds or removes classes depending on their current state

These methods help change styling dynamically based on interactions or logic.

Toggling Classes
$(document).ready(function() {
  $("#themeBtn").click(function() {
    // Toggles a CSS class called 'dark-mode' on the body element
    $("body").toggleClass("dark-mode");
  });
});

title: css() Method
content:
jQuery css() Method

Direct Styling

The css() method is used to set or return one or more CSS properties for the selected elements. You can retrieve the value of a style property or apply new style values to multiple elements using this method.

Setting CSS
$(document).ready(function() {
  $("#colorBtn").click(function() {
    $("p").css("color", "red");
  });
});

This lets you dynamically update styling through jQuery.

title: Insert Elements
content:
jQuery Add/Insert Elements

Building the DOM

jQuery provides several methods to insert new HTML content relative to selected elements:



  • append() – Inserts content at the end of matched elements
  • prepend() – Inserts at the beginning
  • after() – Inserts content after selected elements
  • before() – Inserts content before selected elements

These methods let you dynamically build or modify page content.

title: Final Summary
content:
jQuery HTML/CSS Summary 🚀

Styling Mastered! 🏆

This section added more jQuery skills including advanced HTML and CSS manipulation methods like adding/removing/toggling classes, changing style values, and inserting new elements into the DOM. Combined with selectors, events, effects, AJAX, and traversing, these tools let you create robust and interactive web pages with jQuery.

title: noConflict()
content:
jQuery noConflict() Method

Releasing the $ Alias

When using multiple JavaScript libraries that use the $ shortcut, jQuery’s noConflict() method releases the $ identifier so other libraries can use it. This method helps avoid conflicts when multiple frameworks are used on the same page.



After calling $.noConflict(), you can still use jQuery with the jQuery name:

Using the Full Name
$.noConflict();
jQuery(document).ready(function(){
  jQuery("button").click(function(){
    jQuery("p").text("jQuery still works without the $ sign!");
  });
});

title: Filters
content:
jQuery Filters

Narrowing the Search

jQuery filters let you reduce a set of selected elements based on specific criteria. Filters are useful when you need to narrow down element selections for further operations.



For example, you can return only those elements that match a condition using methods like filter().

Basic Filtering
$(document).ready(function() {
  $("#filterBtn").click(function() {
    // Selects all paragraphs, but ONLY returns the ones with the 'intro' class
    $("p").filter(".intro").css("background-color", "yellow");
  });
});

title: filter() Method
content:
jQuery filter() Method

Advanced Criteria

The filter() method returns elements from a selection that match a given criteria. Elements that do not match are excluded. This is often used after selecting a group of elements to refine results based on conditions.

Filtering with Functions
$(document).ready(function() {
  $("#advancedFilterBtn").click(function() {
    // You can pass a function to filter() for complex logic
    $("p").filter(function(index) {
      // Only return paragraphs that have more than 50 characters of text
      return $(this).text().length > 50;
    }).css("border", "2px solid red");
  });
});

title: Misc Summary
content:
jQuery Miscellaneous Summary 🚀

jQuery Complete! 🏆

In this section, you learned about miscellaneous jQuery features like using noConflict() to avoid conflicts with other libraries, and using filters to select subset elements based on criteria.

These skills help make your jQuery code more robust and flexible when working with complex pages and other JavaScript frameworks.

title: :first Selector
content:
jQuery :first Selector

The First Match

The :first selector selects the very first matching element in a set of elements. It is useful when you want to operate only on the first occurrence of an element type. This selector works on any element and helps target a specific element when multiple elements match a general selector.

Using :first
$(document).ready(function() {
  // Selects only the first <p> element
  $("p:first").css("background-color", "yellow");
});

title: :contains() Selector
content:
jQuery :contains() Selector

Text Filtering

The :contains(text) selector finds elements that contain the specified text. This is useful for filtering lists or paragraphs that contain certain words. It’s a simple way to filter elements based on their text content.



Using :contains()
$(document).ready(function() {
  // Selects all <p> that contain the word “Hello”
  $("p:contains('Hello')").css("font-weight", "bold");
});

title: Keyboard Events
content:
Keyboard & Focus Events

Form Interaction

jQuery supports events related to keyboard input and field focus, which help make forms interactive:



  • keypress() – fires when a key is pressed
  • keydown() – fires when a key is pressed down
  • keyup() – fires when a key is released
  • focus() – triggers when an element gains focus
  • blur() – triggers when an element loses focus

These events are essential for handling form behavior and user input dynamically.

title: AJAX Details
content:
jQuery AJAX Detailed Methods

Advanced Fetching

jQuery provides multiple methods to perform AJAX (background server communication) easily without page reloads:

  • $.ajax() – main method for custom AJAX calls
  • $.get() – GET request to load data
  • $.post() – POST request to send and receive data
  • $.getJSON() – loads JSON data

These methods let you load content, update HTML, and interact with APIs without refreshing the page.

title: Final Summary
content:
jQuery Final Summary 🚀

Curriculum Complete! 🏆

This concludes your jQuery tutorial based on the official W3Schools jQuery syllabus. In this course, you learned how to include and use the jQuery library, how to select and manipulate DOM elements, how to handle events such as clicks and keyboard interactions, and how to apply visual effects and animations.

You also explored methods for getting and setting HTML content and attributes, dynamically changing CSS styles, traversing the DOM tree, and using AJAX to communicate with servers without reloading the page. Additionally, advanced utilities like filters, noConflict mode, and custom selectors were covered to help you refine your selections and avoid library conflicts.

This section expanded your jQuery skills with advanced selectors like :first and :contains(), keyboard and focus event handling, and detailed AJAX methods such as $.get(), $.post(), and $.ajax().

Combined with your prior lessons on selectors, events, effects, DOM manipulation, AJAX basics, and miscellaneous utilities, you now have a comprehensive foundation in jQuery that follows the full W3Schools jQuery curriculum. With these skills, you are ready to build rich interactive front-end web applications using jQuery. Continue practicing with examples and exercises on W3Schools to reinforce your knowledge.
